questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman undergoes CT of the abdomen, and the radiologist notes the spleen in the left upper quadrant. In relation to the surrounding organs, where is the spleen normally located?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anterior to the stomach, anterior to the left kidney, lateral to the pancreas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anterior to the stomach, posterior to the left kidney, medial to the pancreas

choices.label: C
choices.text: Posterior to the stomach, anterior to the left kidney, lateral to the pancreas

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posterior to the stomach, posterior to the left kidney, medial to the pancreas

choices.label: E
choices.text: Posterior to the stomach, anterior to the left kidney, medial to the pancreas

explanation.summary: The spleen sits in the left upper quadrant, posterior to the stomach, anterior to the left kidney, and lateral to the pancreas.
explanation.whyCorrect: The spleen lies almost entirely intraperitoneally in the left upper quadrant, shielded by ribs 9 to 11. It sits behind (posterior to) the stomach and just beneath the diaphragm, with the tail of the pancreas reaching its hilum, so the spleen is lateral to the pancreas and anterior to the left kidney.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The spleen is posterior, not anterior, to the stomach.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The spleen is anterior to the left kidney and lateral (not medial) to the pancreas, and it lies posterior to the stomach.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The spleen is anterior to the left kidney and lateral to the pancreas, not posterior and medial.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The spleen is lateral, not medial, to the pancreas.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man undergoes splenectomy after a motor vehicle collision ruptures his spleen. His physician emphasizes vaccination and counsels him that, without a spleen, he is at increased risk for severe infection. Infection with which of the following types of organisms is he now most susceptible to?

explanation.summary: People can live without a spleen but become especially susceptible to severe infections from encapsulated bacteria.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that in the absence of the spleen, individuals become more susceptible to severe infections, especially from encapsulated bacteria. The white pulp and marginal zone are critical for tagging and clearing encapsulated organisms such as pneumococci, so their loss leaves the patient vulnerable to these bacteria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The spleen's protective role highlighted in the brick is against encapsulated bacteria, not enveloped viruses.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dermatophyte fungal infections are not the classic post-splenectomy risk described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The heightened risk is specifically from encapsulated organisms, not non-encapsulated gram-negative rods.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Helminth infections are not the characteristic susceptibility that follows loss of the spleen.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy with sickle cell disease comes in with pain and a full feeling in his upper abdomen for one week. Physical exam shows an enlarged spleen, and his hematocrit and red blood cell count are mildly reduced. Which of the following best explains why his spleen is enlarged?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neoplastic infiltration of the spleen by lymphocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased splenic removal of damaged red blood cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Storage of an excess number of platelets

choices.label: E
choices.text: A bacterial abscess within the spleen

explanation.summary: In hemolytic diseases such as sickle cell disease, the spleen enlarges because it must remove large numbers of damaged red blood cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: One function of the spleen is to remove damaged RBCs from the circulation. In sickle cell disease the abnormal RBCs are continually damaged and must be cleared, putting the spleen into overload and causing it to enlarge. This matches his mildly reduced hematocrit and RBC count.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neoplastic lymphocyte infiltration occurs in some leukemias, but nothing in this sickle cell presentation points to a malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Portal hypertension causes congestive splenomegaly through venous backpressure, but this patient has a hemolytic condition, not liver disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Splenic enlargement tends to lower the platelet count by storing platelets; excess platelet storage is not the cause of splenomegaly here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no fever or other evidence of a splenic abscess in this hemolytic presentation.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A histologic section of the splenic red pulp shows extravascular spaces packed with tissue macrophages that are engulfing aged and damaged red blood cells. Which of the following structures is being described?

explanation.summary: The splenic cords are extravascular spaces of the red pulp containing macrophages that phagocytose old or damaged RBCs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Splenic cords (cords of Billroth) are extravascular structures that contain tissue macrophages responsible for phagocytosing damaged or old RBCs, along with reticular cells and connective tissue. During open circulation, RBCs percolate through these cords where the macrophages remove defective cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The germinal center is at the center of a white pulp follicle and contains activated B cells, not RBC-clearing macrophages.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The periarteriolar lymphoid sheath is a white pulp region rich in T cells, not the site of RBC phagocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Splenic sinusoids are the leaky vessels that return healthy RBCs to the circulation; the macrophage-filled extravascular space is the cords.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The marginal zone lies in the white pulp at the border of the follicle and screens blood, rather than serving as the main RBC-phagocytosing space.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: In the splenic red pulp, older and inflexible red blood cells cannot squeeze through the narrow slit-like openings between the elongated endothelial cells lining the sinusoids, whereas flexible cells pass through and re-enter the circulation. Which of the following cells creates this physical filter?

explanation.summary: Stave cells are the elongated endothelial cells that line splenic sinusoids and form slit-like openings testing RBC flexibility.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sinusoids of the red pulp are lined by elongated endothelial cells called stave cells, surrounded by reticular fibers in a barrel-like fashion to create small slit-like openings. Only flexible (nonsenescent) RBCs can pass through these slits; older, inflexible cells are retained and phagocytosed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tissue macrophages phagocytose the retained damaged RBCs, but they do not form the slit-like filter of the sinusoid wall.
whyWrongByChoice.B: T lymphocytes reside in the white pulp and have no role in the physical RBC filter of the red pulp.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reticular cells make the reticular (collagen III) fibers that surround the stave cells, but the elongated endothelial cells forming the slits are the stave cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Megakaryocytes produce platelets and are not part of the sinusoidal filter.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: The splenic vein exits the spleen at the hilum and courses toward the liver, where it merges with the superior mesenteric vein. This union forms which of the following vessels?

explanation.summary: The splenic vein joins the superior mesenteric vein to form the portal vein.
explanation.whyCorrect: The splenic vein courses toward the liver and ends by merging with the superior mesenteric vein to form the portal vein, which then carries blood toward the liver.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The inferior vena cava is a systemic vein and is not formed by the splenic and superior mesenteric veins.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The inferior mesenteric vein is a separate tributary of the portal system, not the vessel formed by this union.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The hepatic veins drain blood out of the liver into the inferior vena cava; they are not formed at this junction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The celiac trunk is an artery arising from the aorta, not a vein formed by this merger.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis is found on exam to have an enlarged spleen. Which of the following mechanisms best explains his splenomegaly?

explanation.summary: In portal hypertension, elevated portal vein pressure backs up into the splenic vein and produces congestive splenomegaly.
explanation.whyCorrect: Portal hypertension, an increase in portal vein pressure due to hepatic disease such as cirrhosis, causes increased backpressure transmitted to the splenic vein. This raised pressure may cause fluid to leak from the splenic vein and lead to splenic enlargement, termed congestive splenomegaly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A splenic artery aneurysm is an arterial outpouching and is not the mechanism of enlargement in cirrhosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Neoplastic lymphocyte infiltration occurs in some leukemias, but this patient's liver disease drives venous congestion instead.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Clearing encapsulated bacteria is an immune function of the spleen and does not explain congestive enlargement from cirrhosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thoracic duct obstruction affects lymph drainage systemically and is not the described cause of congestive splenomegaly.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: The splenic artery travels within the splenorenal ligament and enters the spleen at the hilum. This artery is a direct branch of which of the following vessels?

explanation.summary: The splenic artery is a branch of the celiac artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: The splenic artery is a branch of the celiac artery, which is the first major arterial branch of the abdominal aorta. It travels in the splenorenal ligament to enter the spleen at the hilum.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The superior mesenteric artery is a separate branch of the aorta supplying the midgut, not the origin of the splenic artery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The left renal artery supplies the kidney and does not give rise to the splenic artery.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The inferior mesenteric artery supplies the hindgut and is unrelated to the splenic artery.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The splenic artery arises from the celiac artery, not directly from the aorta.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A histologic section of the splenic white pulp shows a sheath of lymphocytes wrapped around a central arteriole. Which of the following cell types predominates in this region?

explanation.summary: The periarteriolar lymphoid sheath (PALS) surrounds the central arteriole and is rich in T cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sheath of lymphocytes around the central arteriole is the periarteriolar lymphoid sheath (PALS), which mostly contains T cells. T cells are delivered to the PALS by the central arteriole and are activated against invading antigens or neoplastic cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: B lymphocytes predominate in the splenic follicles, not in the sheath directly surrounding the central arteriole.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Macrophages are found in the splenic cords and marginal zone, not as the predominant cell of the PALS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neutrophils are not the resident lymphocyte population of the white pulp PALS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Plasma cells derive from activated B cells and are not the defining population of the PALS.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man is diagnosed with streptococcal pneumonia. In his spleen, the antibody-producing cells that help fight this encapsulated organism reside mainly in which of the following regions?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Splenic follicles of the white pulp

explanation.summary: B cells that produce antibodies reside in the splenic follicles of the white pulp.
explanation.whyCorrect: The periphery of the white pulp contains numerous splenic follicles largely made up of B lymphocytes, which are responsible for producing antibodies as part of the immune response. These follicles (including the germinal center, mantle zone, and marginal zone) are the main site of the antibody response against encapsulated bacteria such as pneumococci.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The splenic cords are red pulp structures involved in phagocytosing damaged RBCs, not antibody production.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Splenic sinusoids are blood vessels that return healthy RBCs to the circulation, not sites of B-cell antibody production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The periarteriolar lymphoid sheath is rich in T cells, not the antibody-producing B cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trabeculae are fibrous septa carrying vessels; they do not house antibody-producing cells.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with massive splenomegaly is found to have a low platelet count, although her bone marrow shows normal numbers of megakaryocytes. Which of the following best explains her low platelet count?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased megakaryocytes in the bone marrow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dilution of platelets by intravenous fluids

choices.label: E
choices.text: A congenital defect in platelet granules

explanation.summary: The spleen stores platelets, so an enlarged spleen sequesters platelets and can lower the circulating platelet count.
explanation.whyCorrect: One function of the spleen is to store platelets, and the brick notes that enlargement of the spleen can lower the platelet count. In splenomegaly, a greater fraction of platelets is sequestered in the spleen, reducing the number in circulation even when marrow production is normal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Her marrow shows normal megakaryocytes, so decreased production is not the explanation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased platelet production would tend to raise, not lower, the platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no indication of fluid dilution, and this would not selectively explain splenomegaly-associated thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A granule defect affects platelet function, not the circulating platelet count driven here by splenic sequestration.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: The splenic capsule is a layer of dense fibroelastic tissue that can contract to discharge stored red blood cells into the circulation and stretch to accommodate incoming blood. Which of the following cells within the capsule provides this contractile function?

explanation.summary: The splenic capsule contains contractile cells called myofibroblasts that let it discharge stored RBCs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The splenic capsule is dense fibroelastic tissue that also contains contractile cells called myofibroblasts. Their contraction discharges RBCs into the circulation, and the capsule can also stretch to accommodate incoming blood cells as needed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Stave cells are the elongated endothelial cells lining the splenic sinusoids, not contractile cells of the capsule.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reticular cells produce the reticular fibers of the red pulp; they do not provide the capsule's contractile function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tissue macrophages phagocytose damaged RBCs in the cords rather than contracting the capsule.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dendritic cells participate in the immune response in the marginal zone, not in capsular contraction.

questions.topicName: Structure and Function of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a long history of alcohol use disorder presents with abdominal fullness. Examination reveals ascites, spider angiomata, and a spleen palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin. Laboratory studies show thrombocytopenia and elevated liver function tests. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of his splenomegaly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased splenic vein pressure from portal hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deposition of amyloid protein within the spleen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Infiltration of the spleen by leukemic cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Entrapment of rigid, sickled red blood cells

explanation.summary: Chronic liver disease causes splenomegaly through vascular congestion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cirrhosis raises portal venous pressure due to scarring within the liver. This is transmitted downstream to the splenic vein, increasing splenic pressure and blood volume, which enlarges the spleen. Sequestration of platelets in the congested spleen explains the thrombocytopenia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Amyloid deposition causes splenomegaly through infiltration and is seen in amyloidosis, not as a consequence of cirrhosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Infiltration by leukemic cells is a mechanism seen in hematopoietic malignancy, not in a patient whose findings point to chronic liver disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Red blood cell entrapment causes splenomegaly in hemolytic anemias such as sickle cell disease, not cirrhosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Extramedullary hematopoiesis occurs when the bone marrow fails, as in myelofibrosis, and is not the driver here.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 28-year-old restrained driver is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision. He reports left upper quadrant pain. His heart rate is 88/min and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. He is alert with strong pulses and intact breath sounds. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: C
choices.text: CT scan of the abdomen with contrast

explanation.summary: In a hemodynamically stable trauma patient, CT with contrast is the preferred test to evaluate suspected splenic injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has LUQ pain after blunt trauma but is hemodynamically stable with a normal heart rate and blood pressure. A stable patient can safely undergo CT with contrast, which best defines the presence and extent of splenic injury.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bedside ultrasound is used to screen hemodynamically unstable patients, where time is critical, not stable patients who can tolerate CT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Plain abdominal x-rays are not useful for diagnosing splenic abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Exploratory laparotomy is reserved for unstable patients or those with peritoneal signs; this stable patient first needs imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Embolization or splenectomy are treatments considered after imaging identifies the injury, not the initial step.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman underwent surgical splenectomy after a splenic rupture during a motor vehicle accident. Vaccination against which of the following types of pathogens is most important in this patient?

explanation.summary: Asplenic patients are at greatest risk from encapsulated bacteria and must be vaccinated against them.
explanation.whyCorrect: The spleen produces antibodies that opsonize encapsulated organisms for immune destruction. Without a spleen, patients are at high risk of overwhelming sepsis from Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae type B, and Neisseria meningitidis, so vaccination against these encapsulated bacteria is essential.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vaccination against DNA viruses is not the priority in asplenia; the key threat is encapsulated bacteria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fungal infection is a concern mainly in immunocompromised hosts and is not the priority vaccination in asplenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nonencapsulated bacteria are effectively cleared without splenic opsonization of a capsule, so they are far less dangerous than encapsulated organisms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Parasites are not the encapsulated organisms that the spleen specifically defends against.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: During an anatomy dissection, a medical student identifies a small, red nodule adjacent to the stomach. Cross-section of the nodule demonstrates both red pulp and white pulp. Which of the following is the identified nodule?

explanation.summary: An accessory spleen is the most common congenital splenic anomaly and contains red and white pulp like normal spleen.
explanation.whyCorrect: An accessory spleen is extra, healthy splenic tissue separate from the main body of the spleen. It is usually asymptomatic and found incidentally, and, like a normal spleen, it is composed of white pulp and red pulp.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A lymph node has a cortex and medulla with follicles, not red and white splenic pulp.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A kidney is not this small and shows a cortex and medulla on cross-section rather than splenic pulp.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pancreas has a tan/beige gross and cross-sectional appearance and lacks red and white pulp.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The adrenal gland shows a cortex and medulla, not the red and white pulp characteristic of splenic tissue.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with sickle cell disease has a peripheral blood smear showing red blood cells containing small, dark, round nuclear remnants (Howell-Jolly bodies). This finding most strongly indicates which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The spleen is absent or not functioning properly

choices.label: B
choices.text: The spleen is enlarged and hyperfunctioning

explanation.summary: Howell-Jolly bodies signal that the spleen is absent or nonfunctional.
explanation.whyCorrect: The spleen normally removes red blood cells with abnormalities, including retained nuclear DNA remnants. When Howell-Jolly bodies persist in the circulation, it indicates the spleen is absent or not functioning, as occurs in sickle cell disease after splenic auto-infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A hyperfunctioning spleen would clear these DNA remnants; their presence indicates loss of splenic function, not overactivity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Iron deficiency produces microcytic, hypochromic cells, not Howell-Jolly bodies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes macrocytosis and hypersegmented neutrophils, not this splenic-clearance finding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Extramedullary hematopoiesis reflects blood cell production outside the marrow and does not by itself explain Howell-Jolly bodies.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with long-standing splenomegaly is found to have anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia. Her bone marrow biopsy is normal and shows no infiltrating cells. Which of the following best explains her low blood counts?

explanation.summary: Hypersplenism is an overactive spleen that removes blood cells faster than normal, causing cytopenias.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypersplenism is a complication of splenomegaly in which an overactive spleen removes blood cells faster than normal, decreasing one or more cell lines. This produces anemia, thrombocytopenia with bleeding risk, and leukopenia with infection risk, consistent with a normal marrow.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The marrow biopsy is normal with no infiltrating cells, excluding marrow infiltration as the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blood loss would cause anemia but does not explain the simultaneous drop in platelets and white cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemodilution does not selectively lower all three cell lines through active destruction in an enlarged spleen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic changes rather than pancytopenia driven by an enlarged, overactive spleen.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with infective endocarditis develops fever, left upper quadrant pain, and leukocytosis. A CT scan of the abdomen shows a fluid-filled collection within an enlarged spleen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A splenic abscess typically arises from bacteremia complicating infective endocarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Splenic abscesses are intrasplenic pus collections that most often result from systemic bacteremia, frequently as a complication of infective endocarditis. Patients present with fever, LUQ pain, splenomegaly, and leukocytosis, and CT is the gold standard showing an intrasplenic collection.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Splenic rupture follows trauma and presents with hemorrhage and hemodynamic changes, not an infectious pus collection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An accessory spleen is asymptomatic normal splenic tissue, not a febrile fluid collection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Physiologic asplenia is a small, nonfunctional spleen from auto-infarction and does not present with an abscess cavity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amyloid infiltration enlarges the spleen through protein deposition and does not produce fever and a pus collection.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is evaluated after a bicycle crash in which he struck his left flank. He has left upper quadrant tenderness and reports pain in his left shoulder, though the shoulder itself is uninjured. Which of the following best explains the shoulder pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation due to splenic injury

explanation.summary: Left shoulder pain after blunt trauma (Kehr sign) suggests splenic rupture with diaphragmatic irritation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with splenic rupture may present with referred pain to the left shoulder, known as the Kehr sign, caused by irritation of the diaphragm from intra-abdominal blood. In a patient with LUQ tenderness after left flank trauma, this strongly suggests splenic injury.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A clavicle fracture would produce local bony tenderness and deformity, not referred pain with LUQ tenderness.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocardial infarction is not the expected cause of shoulder pain in a young trauma patient with abdominal findings.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pneumonia would present with respiratory symptoms and fever rather than post-traumatic LUQ tenderness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A rotator cuff strain causes pain with shoulder movement, not referred pain linked to abdominal trauma.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with sickle cell disease undergoes abdominal CT for unrelated pain. The scan reveals a small, shrunken, calcified spleen. Which of the following processes best explains this appearance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Repeated vaso-occlusive infarction leading to auto-infarction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prior surgical removal of the spleen

explanation.summary: In sickle cell disease, recurrent vaso-occlusive crises cause splenic auto-infarction and physiologic asplenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sickle cell disease initially causes splenomegaly as sickled RBCs are trapped, but over time repeated vaso-occlusive crises form small clots in the splenic blood supply. The organ shrinks to a fibrous, calcified, nonfunctional remnant through auto-infarction, producing physiologic asplenia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The imaging shows a shrunken, calcified spleen that is still present, not an absent, surgically removed organ.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Congenital absence would mean no spleen is present, whereas a small calcified remnant is visualized here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amyloid infiltration enlarges the spleen with protein deposits rather than shrinking it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Extramedullary hematopoiesis enlarges the spleen through blood cell production, the opposite of this shrunken appearance.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is brought in after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. Her heart rate is 130/min and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. Her abdomen is distended, and a bedside ultrasound shows free intra-abdominal fluid. Which of the following is the best next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT scan of the abdomen with contrast

explanation.summary: A hemodynamically unstable trauma patient with free intra-abdominal fluid needs urgent operative exploration.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hemodynamically unstable patients, time is critical. A bedside ultrasound that detects intra-abdominal fluid indicates ongoing splenic bleeding, and a diagnostic laparotomy should be performed urgently to control the hemorrhage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT with contrast is reserved for stable patients; an unstable patient cannot safely wait for it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Observation is appropriate only for stable patients without peritoneal signs, not for shock with free fluid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Outpatient follow-up is dangerous in a patient who is actively bleeding and in shock.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endovascular embolization is used in more stable patients with slow bleeding, not in acute hemodynamic instability.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with primary myelofibrosis is found to have massive splenomegaly. His bone marrow is fibrotic and cannot adequately produce blood cells. Which of the following mechanisms best explains his splenic enlargement?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Entrapment of rigid red blood cells from hemolysis

explanation.summary: When the marrow fails, blood cell production shifts to the spleen, causing extramedullary hematopoiesis and splenomegaly.
explanation.whyCorrect: In disease states such as primary myelofibrosis, the bone marrow cannot adequately synthesize blood cells, so hematopoiesis (especially RBC production) occurs in the spleen. This extramedullary hematopoiesis enlarges the organ.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Portal hypertension enlarges the spleen through vascular congestion and would present with liver disease findings, not marrow fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Amyloid deposition is a form of infiltration seen in amyloidosis, not the driver in myelofibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Red cell entrapment causes splenomegaly in hemolytic anemias, not in marrow failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An intrasplenic abscess is a localized infection, not the mechanism of diffuse enlargement in myelofibrosis.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman is suspected of having an enlarged spleen on physical examination. Her physician wants to confirm the finding and accurately measure the size of the spleen. Which of the following is the best test for this purpose?

explanation.summary: Abdominal ultrasound is the best test for measuring the size of the spleen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Splenomegaly can be confirmed with imaging, and abdominal ultrasound is the best test for measuring the size of the spleen. It is less useful than CT/MRI for internal detail, but for sizing the organ it is the modality of choice.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Plain abdominal x-rays are not useful for diagnosing or sizing splenic abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A PET scan evaluates metabolic activity and is not the standard tool for measuring spleen size.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Splenic biopsy carries bleeding risk and is reserved for unknown diagnoses, not for measuring size.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Angiography assesses blood vessels and is not used to measure the size of the spleen.

questions.topicName: Disorders of the Spleen

questions.stem: A first-year medical student is examining an H&E-stained section of a child's thymus at low power. In the paler central region of a lobe, she notes a distinctive spherical structure composed of keratinized epithelial reticular cells arranged in concentric layers. Within which of the following regions of the thymus is this structure located?

explanation.summary: Thymic (Hassall) corpuscles are found in the medulla of the thymus.
explanation.whyCorrect: The structure described is a thymic corpuscle (Hassall corpuscle), a collection of keratinized epithelial reticular cells layered concentrically. These corpuscles are located within the medulla, the paler central region of each thymic lobe.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The capsule is a thin fibrous outer covering and does not contain thymic corpuscles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The corticomedullary junction is the border where mature T cells exit through postcapillary venules, not the site of thymic corpuscles.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The cortex is the densely packed outer region; thymic corpuscles are a medullary feature.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The interlobular septa are fibrous extensions of the capsule that divide the cortex into lobules and contain no thymic corpuscles.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A physiology instructor asks a student to summarize the single main function of the thymus in the immune system. Which of the following best describes this function?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Differentiation of B cells into plasma cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removal of aged and damaged erythrocytes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regulation of the basal metabolic rate

explanation.summary: The thymus's one main function is the production of mature T cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thymus serves as a training ground where T-cell precursors from the bone marrow undergo positive and negative selection to become mature T cells capable of participating in the adaptive immune response.

whyWrongByChoice.B: B-cell differentiation occurs in the bone marrow and lymphoid tissues, not the thymus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Removal of aged and damaged erythrocytes is a function of the spleen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Regulation of the basal metabolic rate is a function of the thyroid gland, not the thymus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antibodies are secreted by plasma cells derived from B cells; mature T cells can potentiate this but the thymus itself does not produce antibodies.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of drooping eyelids and generalized weakness that worsens toward the end of the day. Testing confirms myasthenia gravis, and a chest x-ray reveals an anterior mediastinal mass. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the mass?

explanation.summary: Myasthenia gravis with an anterior mediastinal mass is classically associated with a thymoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: About half of patients with a thymoma also have myasthenia gravis, an autoimmune disease with antibodies against the neuromuscular junction. Because this form of myasthenia is potentially curable, a chest CT is done in all newly diagnosed patients to look for a thymoma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Teratomas can present as thymic masses but are not associated with myasthenia gravis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thymic carcinoma is a rapidly growing thymic malignancy and is not the classic association with myasthenia gravis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymphomas (such as T-cell lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma) can occur in the thymus but are not linked to myasthenia gravis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymic hyperplasia reflects rapid growth of thymic cells and is not the classic cause of a mass in a patient with myasthenia gravis.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A newborn is noted to have a cleft palate, a holosystolic murmur, and poor muscle tone. A chest x-ray shows an absent thymic shadow, and serum calcium is low. Which of the following best explains the underlying developmental defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A chromosome 22 deletion causing abnormal development of the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches

choices.label: B
choices.text: An X-linked recessive mutation in the WAS gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autoantibodies directed against the neuromuscular junction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Failure of positive selection of T cells in the thymic cortex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Age-related loss of thymic cells at roughly 3% per year

explanation.summary: DiGeorge syndrome results from a chromosome 22 deletion that disrupts neural crest-derived development of the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches.
explanation.whyCorrect: DiGeorge syndrome is a congenital disorder caused by a gene deletion on chromosome 22 that leads to failure of normal neural crest development, producing abnormal third and fourth pharyngeal pouches. This causes thymic hypoplasia (absent thymic shadow), parathyroid hypoplasia (hypocalcemia), and congenital heart disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A WAS gene mutation causes Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, characterized by eczema, thrombocytopenia, and recurrent infections, not this pharyngeal pouch phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Autoantibodies against the neuromuscular junction cause myasthenia gravis, which is associated with thymoma rather than congenital thymic hypoplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Failure of positive selection describes a step in normal T-cell development, not the congenital cause of an absent thymus with hypocalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Age-related thymic hypoplasia occurs gradually in adulthood and does not explain a congenital defect in a newborn.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A pathology resident compares the cortex and medulla of a thymus on an H&E-stained slide. The medulla appears distinctly paler than the darkly basophilic cortex. Which of the following best explains why the medulla stains more lightly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It contains fewer densely packed lymphocytes and more epithelial cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its lymphocytes are more tightly packed than those of the cortex

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is composed mostly of fatty tissue that has replaced lymphocytes

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is surrounded by a thick fibrous capsule that blocks staining

choices.label: E
choices.text: It lacks any epithelial reticular cells

explanation.summary: The medulla is paler because it has fewer densely packed lymphocytes and more epithelial cells, whose cytoplasm is eosinophilic.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cortex appears dark blue (basophilic) because lymphocytes are packed closely together. The medulla contains fewer densely packed lymphocytes and more epithelial cells, which have more eosinophilic cytoplasm, giving it a paler appearance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Lymphocytes are more tightly packed in the cortex, which is why the cortex, not the medulla, stains darkly.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fatty replacement occurs gradually with age throughout the whole thymus and is not the reason the medulla is paler than the cortex on a childhood section.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The fibrous capsule surrounds the whole organ and does not selectively lighten medullary staining.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The medulla is actually rich in epithelial cells, including those forming thymic corpuscles.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: An immunology student is studying the blood-thymus barrier, which makes it nearly impossible for circulating antigens in the blood to enter the thymus. Which of the following best explains why maintaining this barrier is important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Circulating antigens would disrupt the normal process of T-cell selection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Circulating antigens would trigger fatty replacement of the thymus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Circulating antigens would prevent B cells from entering the medulla

choices.label: D
choices.text: Circulating antigens would cause the thymus to fuse with the thyroid gland

choices.label: E
choices.text: Circulating antigens would stimulate excessive antibody production by thymocytes

explanation.summary: The blood-thymus barrier keeps blood-borne antigens out so they cannot disrupt T-cell selection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The barrier is formed by epithelial-reticular cells joined by tight junctions and nonfenestrated thymic cortical capillaries. It prevents circulating antigens from entering the thymus, because exposure to such antigens would disrupt the normal positive and negative selection of developing T cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Fatty replacement of the thymus is an age-related process, not a consequence of antigen exposure across the barrier.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The barrier's purpose is to protect T-cell selection, not to control B-cell entry into the medulla.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no mechanism by which antigens cause the thymus to fuse with the thyroid; severe hyperplasia may merely be mistaken for an enlarged thyroid.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymocytes are developing T cells and do not produce antibodies.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: During T-cell development, a developing T cell in the thymic medulla is presented with a self-antigen by an antigen-presenting cell and binds to it very strongly. Which of the following is the most likely fate of this T cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is destroyed by apoptosis because it is autoreactive

choices.label: B
choices.text: It enters the peripheral circulation as a helper T cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: It differentiates into a regulatory T (Treg) cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: It migrates back to the cortex for positive selection

choices.label: E
choices.text: It matures into a B cell that secretes antibodies

explanation.summary: During negative selection in the medulla, T cells that bind self-antigen too strongly are deemed autoreactive and destroyed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Negative selection occurs in the medulla, where T cells are again presented with self-antigens by antigen-presenting cells. If a T cell binds too strongly, it is deemed autoreactive and destroyed by apoptosis, preventing reactions against the body's own tissues.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Weak binding to self-antigen leads to positive selection and entry into the peripheral circulation, not strong binding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intermediate binding to self-antigen leads a cell to become a regulatory T (Treg) cell, not strong binding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Positive selection occurs earlier in the cortex; cells do not migrate back to the cortex after reaching the medulla.
whyWrongByChoice.E: T cells never become B cells; the two are separate lineages.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A developing T cell has just arrived in the thymic cortex from the bone marrow. It is presented with self-antigens but fails to recognize them. Which of the following is the most likely outcome for this cell?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It progresses inward to the medulla for negative selection

choices.label: C
choices.text: It immediately exits into the systemic circulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: It becomes a cytotoxic CD8+ T cell

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is stored in a thymic corpuscle

explanation.summary: During positive selection in the cortex, T cells that fail to recognize self-antigens undergo apoptosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In positive selection, developing T cells in the cortex are presented with self-antigens. Those that recognize and react to self-antigens progress inward to the medulla, while those that fail to recognize self-antigens undergo apoptosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Only cells that successfully recognize self-antigens progress to the medulla; a cell that fails positive selection does not advance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cells exit into the systemic circulation only after completing both positive and negative selection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Differentiation into CD4+ or CD8+ cells occurs after successful completion of selection, not after failing positive selection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymic corpuscles are concentric collections of keratinized epithelial cells and do not store T cells.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A radiologist notes that the thymus is easily seen as a sharply demarcated, sail-shaped structure on the chest x-ray of a healthy neonate, yet it is not visible on the chest x-ray of a healthy 40-year-old. Which of the following best explains why the thymus becomes less visible in the adult?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The thymus involutes and loses cells while the chest enlarges with growth

choices.label: B
choices.text: The thymus migrates out of the mediastinum into the neck

choices.label: C
choices.text: The thymus is replaced by a rapidly growing thymic carcinoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: The adult thymus becomes densely calcified and radiolucent

choices.label: E
choices.text: The adult thymus is shielded by the developing sternum only after puberty

explanation.summary: The thymus is most visible in the neonate and becomes harder to see in adults as it involutes while the chest grows.
explanation.whyCorrect: The thymus is most easily seen on a neonate's chest x-ray because thymic size is at its maximum while the chest is small. As a child grows, the thymus involutes (losing roughly 3% of its cells per year) and the chest enlarges, making the thymus less visible in adults.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The thymus stays in the superior mediastinum; it does not migrate into the neck with age.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Loss of visibility is a normal aging process, not the result of a thymic carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The adult thymus becomes fatty and shrinks rather than becoming densely calcified.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The sternum lies in front of the thymus at all ages and is not what makes the adult thymus invisible.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with a large anterior mediastinal mass develops swelling of the face and neck, distended veins across his chest, and shortness of breath. Which of the following mechanisms best explains these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obstruction of blood flow through the superior vena cava by the mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deletion of a gene on chromosome 22

choices.label: D
choices.text: Failure of negative selection producing autoreactive T cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced cell-based immunity from age-related thymic involution

explanation.summary: Mediastinal neoplasms can compress the superior vena cava, producing superior vena cava syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mediastinal neoplasms such as thymoma and lymphoma can be associated with superior vena cava syndrome, a constellation of signs that occur when blood flow through the superior vena cava is blocked by the mass. This causes face and neck swelling, upper extremity swelling, dyspnea, cough, and dilated collateral chest veins.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Autoantibodies at the neuromuscular junction cause the muscle weakness of myasthenia gravis, not facial swelling and distended chest veins.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A chromosome 22 deletion causes congenital DiGeorge syndrome, not superior vena cava obstruction in an adult.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Failure of negative selection leads to autoimmune disease, not the venous congestion described here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Age-related thymic involution reduces immune function but does not cause superior vena cava obstruction.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A 10-month-old boy has a history of recurrent ear and sinus infections, widespread eczema, and easy bruising. Laboratory studies show thrombocytopenia with unusually small platelets. This X-linked recessive condition is caused by a mutation in which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A gene encoding a neuromuscular junction receptor

choices.label: D
choices.text: A gene controlling positive selection in the cortex

explanation.summary: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by WAS gene mutations, producing recurrent infections, eczema, and thrombocytopenia with small platelets.
explanation.whyCorrect: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome results from abnormalities of the WAS gene and produces a triad of recurrent infections (from a hypoplastic/aplastic thymus), eczema, and thrombocytopenia with small platelets, matching this patient's presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A chromosome 22 deletion causes DiGeorge syndrome, which features parathyroid hypoplasia and congenital heart disease rather than eczema and small-platelet thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A neuromuscular junction defect underlies myasthenia gravis, which causes muscle weakness, not this triad.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Positive selection is a normal step of T-cell development and is not the gene defect underlying this syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alpha-globin gene defects cause alpha-thalassemia, a red cell disorder unrelated to this immunodeficiency triad.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man has had several respiratory infections over the past year and mounts a weak antibody response to the influenza vaccine. His physician attributes this in part to reduced cell-based immunity. Which of the following age-related changes in the thymus best accounts for this decline?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gradual loss of thymic cells at roughly 3% per year

choices.label: B
choices.text: A rapidly growing thymic carcinoma replacing normal tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blockade of the neuromuscular junction by autoantibodies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Failure of the blood-thymus barrier allowing antigens to enter

explanation.summary: Age-related thymic hypoplasia results from the normal ongoing loss of thymic cells, decreasing cell-based immunity in older adults.
explanation.whyCorrect: Age-related thymic hypoplasia is a consequence of the normal loss of thymic cells throughout adulthood (about 3% per year). This reduced thymic function decreases cell-based immunity, contributing to more infections and malignancies and to decreased responsiveness to vaccines in older patients.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A thymic carcinoma is a malignancy that presents as a mass, not the normal age-related decline described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A chromosome 22 deletion causes congenital DiGeorge syndrome, not a gradual decline appearing in an 82-year-old.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Autoantibody blockade of the neuromuscular junction causes myasthenia gravis, not reduced vaccine responsiveness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Failure of the blood-thymus barrier would disrupt T-cell selection but is not the described mechanism of age-related immune decline.

questions.topicName: Structure, Function, and Disorders of the Thymus

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man receiving chemotherapy develops acute vomiting that has not responded to his scheduled agents. He is given metoclopramide, which reaches the chemoreceptor trigger zone in the medulla. Blockade of which of the following receptors in the area postrema is primarily responsible for this drug's antiemetic effect?

explanation.summary: Metoclopramide is a D2 dopamine antagonist that works at the area postrema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Metoclopramide's antiemetic action comes into play when it reaches the chemoreceptor trigger zone (area postrema) in the medulla. Blocking D2 dopamine receptors there inhibits stimulation of the vomiting center. It also antagonizes 5-HT3 receptors, which adds to its effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Histamine-1 receptors are targeted by antihistamines and are relevant to the vestibular system, not metoclopramide's primary action.
whyWrongByChoice.C: M1 muscarinic receptors are blocked by antimuscarinics such as scopolamine, not by metoclopramide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NK1 substance P receptors are blocked by aprepitant, not metoclopramide.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GABA receptors are not a target of the antiemetic agents described in this brick.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with breast cancer is started on ondansetron before her chemotherapy infusion. In addition to acting on the chemoreceptor trigger zone, this drug reduces nausea by blocking serotonin receptors at which of the following peripheral sites?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vagus nerve terminals in the gastrointestinal tract

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Muscarinic receptors of the inner ear

explanation.summary: 5-HT3 antagonists block serotonin both centrally and on peripheral vagal terminals.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ondansetron, palonosetron, and granisetron are selective 5-HT3 antagonists that block 5-HT3 receptors in the CTZ within the CNS and 5-HT3 receptors on vagus nerve terminals in the peripheral nervous system. Chemotherapy irritates the GI mucosa, causing serotonin release that stimulates these vagal terminals.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sympathetic ganglia are not the site of 5-HT3 antagonist action described in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nicotinic neuromuscular receptors are unrelated to the antiemetic mechanism of ondansetron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-adrenergic receptors in the heart are not targeted; the cardiac concern with these drugs is QT prolongation, not receptor blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inner-ear muscarinic receptors are the target of scopolamine, an antimuscarinic, not ondansetron.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man is scheduled for a chemotherapy infusion known to cause a high incidence of emesis. About 30 minutes before the infusion he is given aprepitant to prevent nausea and vomiting. This drug is not effective on an as-needed basis because of which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It blocks the NK1 receptor to prevent substance P binding

choices.label: C
choices.text: It depletes serotonin stores in the GI mucosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: It stimulates gastric emptying to clear emetogenic drugs

choices.label: E
choices.text: It neutralizes chemotherapy drugs in the bloodstream

explanation.summary: Aprepitant is an NK1 (substance P) antagonist used as pre-treatment, not as a rescue drug.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aprepitant crosses the BBB and selectively blocks the NK1 receptor to inhibit binding of substance P, which normally causes pain, nausea, and vomiting. Because of this preventive mechanism it is not effective as needed; it is given as pre-treatment about 30 minutes before the infusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aprepitant does not inhibit dopamine synthesis; it acts on NK1 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Depleting serotonin stores is not the mechanism; 5-HT3 antagonists block serotonin receptors rather than deplete stores.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increasing gastric emptying describes metoclopramide, not aprepitant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aprepitant does not chemically neutralize chemotherapy drugs in the blood.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A patient with chemotherapy-related nausea that has a strong vestibular component is prescribed scopolamine delivered by a transdermal patch. This agent relieves nausea primarily by blocking which of the following receptors?

explanation.summary: Scopolamine is a nonspecific muscarinic antagonist acting at the M1 receptor.
explanation.whyCorrect: The vestibular system communicates with the nucleus tractus solitarius to induce nausea and vomiting. Scopolamine, an antimuscarinic, crosses the BBB and blocks the M1 receptor to treat motion sickness and can help with chemotherapy-induced nausea. A transdermal patch helps avoid anticholinergic side effects.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 5-HT3 receptors are blocked by ondansetron and related agents, not scopolamine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: D2 dopamine receptors are the target of metoclopramide, not scopolamine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NK1 receptors are the target of aprepitant, not scopolamine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-adrenergic receptors are not involved in scopolamine's antiemetic action.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman receives dexamethasone as part of her pre-treatment regimen before a highly emetogenic chemotherapy infusion. During therapy she is monitored for which of the following adverse effects most commonly associated with corticosteroid use in this setting?

explanation.summary: In chemotherapy-induced nausea, steroids most commonly cause hyperglycemia and GI bleed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone are used as pre-treatment for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting. Although steroids can have many side effects, when used in this setting the most common are hyperglycemia and risk of GI bleed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tardive dyskinesia is a serious CNS effect of metoclopramide, not steroids.
whyWrongByChoice.B: QT-interval prolongation is a rare effect of 5-HT3 antagonists, not corticosteroids.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neutropenia is listed as an uncommon effect of aprepitant, not steroids.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pupillary dilation is an anticholinergic effect of scopolamine, not steroids.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man who has taken metoclopramide for several months for chronic nausea develops involuntary, repetitive movements of his tongue and lips that do not resolve when the drug is stopped. This adverse effect is best classified as which of the following?

explanation.summary: Chronic metoclopramide can cause tardive dyskinesia, which is often irreversible.
explanation.whyCorrect: Metoclopramide can cause severe CNS adverse effects, including extrapyramidal symptoms and tardive dyskinesia, which is often irreversible. The persistent involuntary orofacial movements after stopping the drug fit tardive dyskinesia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Serotonin syndrome is a risk when metoclopramide is combined with SSRIs, but it presents acutely with autonomic and neuromuscular changes, not persistent orofacial dyskinesia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neutropenia is an uncommon effect of aprepitant, not a cause of involuntary movements.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hyperglycemia is a steroid effect and does not cause involuntary movements.
whyWrongByChoice.E: QT-interval prolongation is a 5-HT3 antagonist effect and is a cardiac finding, not a movement disorder.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man taking a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor for depression is being considered for an antiemetic. The team notes that adding one particular agent could precipitate serotonin syndrome, especially in young adults and at high doses. Which of the following drugs is being avoided for this reason?

explanation.summary: Metoclopramide should not be combined with SSRIs due to serotonin syndrome risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states metoclopramide should not be used with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors because of the risk of serotonin syndrome, particularly in children and young adults and at high doses. This reflects metoclopramide's activity at 5-HT3 receptors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aprepitant is an NK1 antagonist and is not linked to serotonin syndrome with SSRIs in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Scopolamine is an antimuscarinic and does not carry this serotonin syndrome caution.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dexamethasone is a corticosteroid and is not associated with serotonin syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prednisone is a corticosteroid, not a serotonergic drug, and does not carry this caution.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman who recently gave birth is struggling to produce breast milk. Her physician notes that one antiemetic increases prolactin secretion and could be used to stimulate lactation, though it is not often used for this purpose because of its other adverse effects. Which of the following best explains how this drug increases milk production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It directly stimulates prolactin receptors in the breast

choices.label: B
choices.text: It blocks dopamine, relieving dopamine's inhibition of prolactin secretion

choices.label: C
choices.text: It blocks 5-HT3 receptors, increasing prolactin release

choices.label: D
choices.text: It suppresses inflammatory mediators that inhibit lactation

choices.label: E
choices.text: It blocks NK1 receptors, raising substance P and prolactin

explanation.summary: Metoclopramide raises prolactin because dopamine normally inhibits prolactin release.
explanation.whyCorrect: Metoclopramide can increase milk production in both males and females. Dopamine naturally inhibits secretion of prolactin, the hormone responsible for milk production; by blocking dopamine, metoclopramide relieves this inhibition and prolactin rises.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metoclopramide does not directly stimulate breast prolactin receptors; it acts by removing dopamine's inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The lactation effect is due to dopamine blockade, not 5-HT3 receptor activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Suppressing inflammatory mediators describes the antiemetic mechanism of steroids, not the prolactin effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocking NK1 receptors describes aprepitant and is unrelated to prolactin secretion.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A patient on ondansetron for chemotherapy-induced nausea is monitored with periodic electrocardiograms. Which of the following is the major, though rare, adverse effect that prompts this monitoring?

explanation.summary: The major but rare adverse effect of 5-HT3 antagonists is QT-interval prolongation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Several adverse effects of the serotonin antagonists include headache, dizziness, and constipation. A major side effect to look out for is prolongation of the QT interval, but this is rare, which is why an ECG may be monitored.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a severe CNS effect of metoclopramide, not ondansetron.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyperglycemia is a corticosteroid effect, not a 5-HT3 antagonist effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pupillary dilation is an anticholinergic effect of scopolamine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tardive dyskinesia is a metoclopramide effect, not an ondansetron effect.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man is about to begin cisplatin, which carries the highest emesis risk of the chemotherapy drugs with an incidence greater than 90%. Following the preferred multi-drug approach, which of the following three-drug antiemetic regimens is most appropriate as a starting point?

explanation.summary: High-emesis-risk cisplatin warrants at least a three-drug regimen of aprepitant, ondansetron, and dexamethasone.
explanation.whyCorrect: A multi-drug regimen is preferred to prevent stimuli from multiple sources. A patient receiving cisplatin (highest emesis risk, incidence >90%) would receive at least a three-drug antiemetic regimen that includes aprepitant, ondansetron, and dexamethasone. Other agents like an antimuscarinic or D2 antagonist may be added if needed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: This regimen omits the recommended NK1 antagonist and 5-HT3 antagonist that anchor the standard cisplatin regimen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This combination lacks a 5-HT3 antagonist and a steroid, which are core components of the recommended regimen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This combination lacks the NK1 antagonist and steroid; scopolamine and metoclopramide are add-ons if needed, not the base regimen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Scopolamine is an add-on for vestibular symptoms; the base triple therapy pairs a steroid and 5-HT3 antagonist with an NK1 antagonist, not scopolamine.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with breast cancer on ongoing chemotherapy reports persistent nausea despite consistent use of ondansetron. Her team wants to add a second agent with a different mechanism for her breakthrough nausea. Because the NK1 antagonist and steroid are used mainly as pre-treatment and an antimuscarinic is best for motion sickness, which of the following is the most appropriate addition?

explanation.summary: For breakthrough nausea in a patient already on a 5-HT3 antagonist, a D2 antagonist is the best add-on.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient already takes ondansetron, a 5-HT3 antagonist. NK1 antagonists (aprepitant) and steroids (dexamethasone) are used primarily as pre-treatment before infusions, and antimuscarinics (scopolamine) are most helpful for motion sickness. D2 antagonists are reserved for breakthrough nausea, so metoclopramide is the best option to add.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aprepitant is used primarily as pre-treatment before the infusion and is not effective on an as-needed basis for breakthrough symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dexamethasone is used mainly as pre-treatment, not as an add-on for breakthrough nausea.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Scopolamine is most helpful for vestibular/motion sickness, which is not the driver of this patient's nausea.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Palonosetron is another 5-HT3 antagonist and shares ondansetron's mechanism, so it would not add a different mechanism.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: During chemotherapy, irritation of the GI mucosa leads to release of a mediator that acts on receptors located in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and on vagal terminals to increase motility and provoke nausea. Which of the following mediators is chiefly responsible?

explanation.summary: Chemotherapy-induced GI irritation causes mucosal serotonin release that drives nausea via 5-HT3 receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chemotherapy can cause GI irritation, which in turn causes mucosal secretion of serotonin in the GI tract; this increases motility and leads to nausea. Serotonin acts on 5-HT3 receptors in the CTZ and on vagal terminals, which is why 5-HT3 antagonists are effective.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Histamine acts at H1 receptors relevant to the vestibular system, not the mucosal serotonin release described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acetylcholine acts at muscarinic receptors and is targeted by antimuscarinics, but it is not the mediator released from irritated GI mucosa here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Substance P acts at NK1 receptors and is blocked by aprepitant, but it is not the GI-mucosal mediator described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dopamine acts at D2 receptors in the area postrema; it is not the mediator released from the irritated GI mucosa.

questions.topicName: Supportive Therapy for Patients on Chemotherapy - Antiemetics

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman returns from a trip to sub-Saharan Africa with high recurring fevers, chills, and myalgia. She now has altered consciousness and a seizure. A Giemsa-stained blood smear shows numerous ring-form trophozoites within red blood cells and a crescent (banana)-shaped gametocyte. Which species of Plasmodium is the most likely cause of her illness?

explanation.summary: Crescent-shaped gametocytes and severe (cerebral) malaria point to P. falciparum.
explanation.whyCorrect: P. falciparum is the most common and deadliest species. Its gametocytes have a distinct crescent (banana) shape, unlike the round gametocytes of other species. It is the species most associated with severe complications such as cerebral malaria, which causes coma and seizures.

whyWrongByChoice.B: P. vivax gametocytes are round rather than crescent-shaped, and it more often causes relapsing rather than severe cerebral disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: P. ovale has round gametocytes and typically causes a milder, relapsing course.
whyWrongByChoice.D: P. malariae has round gametocytes and causes a quartan fever without the crescent gametocyte or cerebral malaria described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: P. knowlesi is a primate parasite responsible for only a small number of cases and does not have crescent-shaped gametocytes.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man who recently traveled to an endemic region develops fever and chills that recur in a regular pattern precisely every 72 hours. A blood smear confirms malaria. Which species of Plasmodium is most consistent with this quartan fever pattern?

explanation.summary: A fever recurring every 72 hours (quartan fever) is characteristic of P. malariae.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because different species have different maturation times within RBCs, the interval between fevers varies by species. P. malariae causes a quartan fever, which occurs every 72 hours as the synchronized parasites rupture RBCs in waves.

whyWrongByChoice.A: P. falciparum initially causes quotidian fever (every 24 hours), later becoming tertian (every 48 hours), not quartan.
whyWrongByChoice.B: P. vivax causes tertian fever every 48 hours.
whyWrongByChoice.D: P. ovale causes tertian fever every 48 hours.
whyWrongByChoice.E: P. knowlesi causes quotidian fever every 24 hours.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man is successfully treated for malaria after a trip abroad. Eight months later, having not traveled since, he develops a new episode of malaria with a fever recurring every 48 hours. Which of the following best explains this relapse so long after the initial infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dormant hypnozoites persisted in the liver and reactivated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gametocytes remained circulating in the bloodstream

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sporozoites remained latent in the skin at the bite site

choices.label: E
choices.text: Trophozoites entered a prolonged ring-form state in RBCs

explanation.summary: Relapse of malaria months to years later is caused by dormant liver hypnozoites of P. vivax and P. ovale.
explanation.whyCorrect: In P. vivax and P. ovale, parasites can reside in the liver in a dormant form called a hypnozoite for days, weeks, or even years. These hypnozoites can eventually reactivate and cause relapse of disease long after the initial infection. The 48-hour (tertian) fever fits P. vivax or P. ovale.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Gametocytes are the sexual form taken up by mosquitoes and fall out of the replication cycle; they do not cause a later relapse.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick does not describe merozoites hiding in splenic macrophages to cause relapse; the dormant reservoir is the liver hypnozoite.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sporozoites travel from the skin to the liver soon after the bite; they do not remain latent in the skin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A prolonged ring-form trophozoite state in RBCs is not the mechanism of relapse; dormancy occurs in the liver as a hypnozoite.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A researcher notes that individuals from certain West African populations whose red blood cells lack a particular surface antigen are essentially resistant to infection by Plasmodium vivax. Absence of which of the following provides this complete protection?

explanation.summary: P. vivax uses the Duffy antigen to enter RBCs, so its absence prevents infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: P. vivax uses the Duffy antigen to enter red blood cells. Absence of the Duffy antigen therefore prevents P. vivax from invading the RBC, providing complete protection against this species.

whyWrongByChoice.B: G6PD deficiency provides partial protection by making infected RBCs prone to oxidative damage, not by blocking a receptor P. vivax needs to enter.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemoglobin S (as in sickle cell trait) makes RBCs less hospitable to the parasite but does not act as the entry receptor for P. vivax.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PfEMP1 is an adhesion molecule expressed by P. falciparum-infected RBCs that binds endothelium; it is not the RBC receptor used by P. vivax.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spectrin is a cytoskeletal protein and is not described as the entry receptor for P. vivax.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A hematologist explains that a patient with sickle cell trait (HbAS) has some natural protection against severe malaria. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of this protection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The abnormal hemoglobin makes red blood cells less hospitable to the parasite

choices.label: B
choices.text: The trait eliminates the Duffy antigen required for parasite entry

choices.label: C
choices.text: The trait prevents mosquitoes from transmitting the parasite

choices.label: D
choices.text: The trait blocks hepatocyte invasion by sporozoites

choices.label: E
choices.text: The trait produces antibodies that neutralize merozoites

explanation.summary: Abnormal hemoglobin in sickle cell trait makes RBCs less hospitable to Plasmodium.
explanation.whyCorrect: In sickle cell trait, one abnormal allele is paired with a normal allele (HbAS), a phenotype that is protective against malaria without causing severe sickle cell disease. The abnormal hemoglobin makes RBCs less hospitable to Plasmodium spp., leading to protection against severe disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Loss of the Duffy antigen is a separate mechanism specific to P. vivax; sickle cell trait works through abnormal hemoglobin, not Duffy antigen loss.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sickle cell trait affects the red blood cell environment, not mosquito transmission.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The protection operates in the RBC stage via abnormal hemoglobin, not by blocking sporozoite invasion of hepatocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The brick attributes protection to the abnormal hemoglobin's effect on the RBC, not to antibody production against merozoites.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A student is tracing the human stage of the Plasmodium life cycle. After an infected Anopheles mosquito bites a person, which form of the parasite is injected and first travels through the bloodstream to invade hepatocytes?

explanation.summary: Sporozoites are injected by the mosquito and travel to the liver to infect hepatocytes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Plasmodium gametocytes within a mosquito develop into sporozoites, the infectious form. During a bite, sporozoites are injected into the human, travel through the bloodstream to the liver, and enter hepatocytes, where they multiply as schizonts.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Merozoites are released from ruptured liver schizonts into the blood and invade RBCs; they are not the form injected by the mosquito.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Trophozoites are the active amoeboid form inside red blood cells, not the form that first enters hepatocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gametocytes are the sexual form taken up by mosquitoes; they do not invade hepatocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The ookinete is a mosquito-stage form and is not the form injected into humans to infect the liver.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A patient with suspected malaria has a complete blood count showing a normocytic/normochromic anemia and an elevated serum lactate dehydrogenase. To confirm the diagnosis and identify the species and parasite burden, which of the following is the gold standard test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microscopy of a Giemsa-stained peripheral blood smear

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid antigen test using monoclonal antibodies

choices.label: D
choices.text: Four-fold rise in antibody titers two weeks apart

choices.label: E
choices.text: Measurement of serum bilirubin and haptoglobin

explanation.summary: The gold standard for diagnosing malaria is microscopy of a Giemsa-stained peripheral blood smear.
explanation.whyCorrect: The gold standard diagnostic test for malaria is microscopy of a Giemsa-stained peripheral blood smear. Smear examination reveals not only the presence of Plasmodium organisms but also the species and the level of parasite burden. The most commonly seen form is the ring-form trophozoite.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Rapid diagnostic tests using monoclonal antibodies are used when microscopy is not available, especially in resource-limited areas, but they are not the gold standard.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blood culture is used to diagnose brucellosis, not malaria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A four-fold rise in antibody titers is a diagnostic approach for brucellosis, not the gold standard for malaria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bilirubin and haptoglobin reflect hemolysis but do not identify the parasite, species, or burden the way the blood smear does.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A global health team is deploying the RTS,S malaria vaccine, now recommended for young children in areas endemic for P. falciparum. This vaccine is a virus-like particle made of a fusion protein consisting of hepatitis B surface antigen and which of the following P. falciparum components?

explanation.summary: The RTS,S vaccine combines the P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein with hepatitis B surface antigen.
explanation.whyCorrect: The only malaria vaccine (RTS,S) is recommended for young children in areas endemic for P. falciparum and has about 30% efficacy in reducing severe disease. It is a virus-like particle made of a fusion protein consisting of the P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein and hepatitis B surface antigen.

whyWrongByChoice.B: PfEMP1 is an adhesion molecule expressed on infected RBCs, not the antigen used in the RTS,S vaccine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no merozoite surface hemoglobinase component in the RTS,S vaccine described in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The vaccine uses the circumsporozoite protein, not a gametocyte antigen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Duffy-binding protein relates to P. vivax RBC entry and is not the component of the RTS,S vaccine.

questions.topicName: Plasmodium spp., Babesia spp., and Brucella spp.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man from Connecticut presents with fever, chills, and fatigue several weeks after a summer hiking trip. Laboratory studies reveal hemolytic anemia, and a Giemsa-stained thin blood smear shows intra-erythrocytic parasites arranged as tetrads resembling a Maltese cross. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Maltese cross tetrads in RBCs after a tick-exposure region point to babesiosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In babesiosis, the merozoites are distinct in that they form tetrads that look like a Maltese cross within red blood cells. Combined with tick exposure in the northeastern US and hemolytic anemia, this is characteristic of infection with Babesia spp.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Malaria parasites form ring-form trophozoites, not Maltese cross tetrads, and are transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes, not ticks in Connecticut.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Brucellosis is caused by gram-negative coccobacilli linked to livestock exposure and does not produce intra-erythrocytic Maltese cross forms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lyme disease is caused by B. burgdorferi and does not appear as intra-RBC tetrads on a blood smear.
